title: Lesson 245
transcript: We've been talking about evaluation criteria and once again evaluation criteria are measurement systems that can be useful to us in the security industry because we can trust what the vendors are telling us because it's not them saying what the security capabilities of their products actually are. It's a measurement system that is independent and objective that tells us the capabilities of those products so that we can trust them We've already talked about how these are very useful, not only for the vendors themselves because it creates confidence in their products, but from a consumer's perspective, we can trust what these evaluations actually tell us because it tells us exactly the security capabilities of products that we might be interested in buying from vendors. Now the latest and the third evaluation criteria system that the industry has come up with is actually called the Common Criteria. The reason it's called the Common Criteria is because it was a common system that was developed by a number of countries that got together and decided to come up with a measurement system that could be trusted anywhere around the world. The idea is you could have a product from a vendor that is rated in Europe but we would trust it in North America simply because it's been done using a rigorous measurement system that is the common criteria itself and what's important for us to remember about the common criteria is that it's the latest system but it's also the best because it addresses a lot of the concerns and the requirements that we may have not only as consumers interested in buying certain products that relate to security from vendors but it also provides vendors with that confidence element that's very important to them. Now what's important for us to understand as far as the exam is concerned is not that it is a ISO standard and what the number is for example but we need to understand the process the flow, if you will, that this provides and how it provides all of that useful information that is meaningful for consumers and for the vendors themselves. But this slide does a really good job of showing us what is referred to as the common criteria flow. And what we need to remember are some of these elements that make up this entire process, this entire flow. And as you can see here, everything starts with something called the protection profile. And if you think about those two words, protection profile, it's simply a profile of what the protection mechanisms should be for a specific type of product. So let's use a firewall as an example. A firewall would have or firewalls would have a protection profile that really starts this process and that profile would simply list all of the security capabilities that any firewall should have. So it would simply list the requirements that any firewall should have. like two-factor authentication, VPNs, encryption capability, secure logging and monitoring. It would simply list what the security capabilities of that product should be. So there's a protection profile that exists for firewalls. That's a category of controls that there would exist a protection profile for. There's a protection profile for access control solutions. There is a protection profile for DLP solutions. There is a protection profile for SIEM solutions. It simply lists what the security capabilities of that type of product should be. Now the target of evaluation or TOE is the next item on your list here. That is simply the product that is being rated. So if we are a vendor and we've designed and built a firewall and we're ready to sell it, we would hire a common criteria licensed organization to come in and look at our product and hopefully if it receives a good rating then people out there are going to buy it again not because we say it's a good product but because the common criteria system itself says it's a good product. I mean that's the whole point of this. But our firewall that is being rated would be referred to in common criteria terms as the TOE, the target of evaluation. It's simply the product that's being rated by the common criteria. It's the product itself. Now the security targets, and this is an important one to remember from an exam perspective, the security targets are each of the specific capabilities that we've built as a vendor, for example, into our firewall so that how does our firewall do VPNs? How does our firewall do encryption capabilities? How does our firewall take care of authentication, how does our firewall take care of logging and monitoring? They're going to have to look at each of those capabilities in our firewall and determine how well they're handled within our specific firewall. This is the part of the common criteria that really creates some meaningful documentation because the licensed organized, the common criteria licensed organization that we've brought in to look at our firewall is gonna have to look at each of the capabilities that we've designed into our firewall. Those are referred to as each of the security targets, because we may have built some really good capabilities into our firewall, but maybe we didn't do so good in some others. All of that will be captured as part of how they look at each of the security targets. And as you can see here, when they're looking at each of the capabilities, security targets, they're gonna have to look at how each of those capabilities address functional, and assurance. You'll remember the two pillars of a well implemented security control, how it needs to address not only functional but also assurance. And so they're going to look at the capabilities, the security targets. That's going to create a lot of documentation. that is going to be meaningful for us because we may choose to put that up on our website so that potential consumers can look at that information and know exactly the capabilities of our firewall and they'll trust all of that documentation because it was created as part of the common criteria process itself. It's not documentation that we created as a vendor to look really good. It's part of the common criteria and it provides objectivity and obviously trust that is very important in how we sell our products. Now once they've looked at all of the capabilities of our firewall, the common criteria works in such a way so that our firewall will receive one overall rating and it's referred to as the EAL level. or evaluation assurance level. Now it's unfortunate that they use the word assurance. The word assurance there is not in reference to just functional and assurance. It's an overall rating that our firewall would get that would tell everybody out there the capabilities and how secure and the capability of our firewall is according to the system that the common criteria has created. And what you're seeing here on this slide are the seven levels that can be achieved. So the idea is every product will receive one of these EAL levels which tells people out there how good our product is. So after they've looked at everything, they will assign one of these ratings or EAL levels and as you can see here it goes from level one which really would mean not very secure all the way up to level seven which means it's been tested and proven to be something that provides security to a really good level. These are the EAL levels that you can get as a rating, a final rating according to the common criteria. Now pay attention here because what's really important from your perspective is to possibly memorize these levels and what they equate to because we know that something that is required as far as the exam is to understand what each of these levels are and you may see a question that could be for example a drag and drop question that asks you, what does EAL Level 1 mean? What does EAL Level 2 mean? In fact, all seven of these. But I think it's pretty easy to understand these. As you can see, for example, Level 7 is talking about a rating that is the best and as you go down we get less testing or less meaningful ratings all the way down to for example EAL1 where we talk about functionally tested so it may be important for you to memorize these and match them up with the different levels that are part of the common criteria.
